metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan a `raw_verdicts.jsonl` schema field (e.g. `replay_index: int`) or specify separate per-replay file naming (e.g. `raw_verdicts_r{1,2,3}.jsonl`) so the three candidate and three current replays are unambiguously distinguished for pairwise null-floor computation and median derivation.
evidence: "capturing each returned verdict dict...to runs/e3/<candidate>/raw_verdicts.jsonl, atomic append per ticket (checkpoint/resume)"
"Scope: main/{raw_verdicts.jsonl, manifest.json}"
"null-replay noise floor is current-vs-current: the median (over the 3 current replays, pairwise) count of plans gaining a new blocking finding"
"run candidate-fresh ×3 and current-fresh ×3 over the recall-stripped clones"
"R1 metrics are taken at the median across the 3 candidate replays"
findings.finding: The N=3 replication design in What requires three separately-identifiable replay outputs (for pairwise null-floor computation on the current/main side), but the Scope lists only a single `raw_verdicts.jsonl` per label (both `main/raw_verdicts.jsonl` and `<candidate>/raw_verdicts.jsonl`), and the append-per-ticket description implies a flat file with no replay-index structure. An implementer has no specification for how to store or distinguish the three replays within that file, making pairwise current-vs-current comparison and per-replay median derivation impossible from the described schema.
findings.location: What: 'The replay (candidate side)' + def 7 (N=3 replicas) vs Scope: `main/{raw_verdicts.jsonl, manifest.json}` and `<candidate>/{raw_verdicts.jsonl, ...}`
scenarios: An implementer appending all 3 replays into one flat `raw_verdicts.jsonl` cannot distinguish which rows belong to which replay, making pairwise comparisons and per-replay medians uncomputable.

The `fp_control.json.replays` array (checked in AC5 as `length==3`) implies 3 separate result sets, but there is no specification of how replay identity is encoded in raw_verdicts.jsonl to feed that array.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'The replay' paragraph, replace the single-file description with: 'Each of the N=3 replays appends to a separate shard `raw_verdicts_r<n>.jsonl` (n∈{1,2,3}) under `<label>/`, with the same checkpoint/resume semantics per shard. `analyze` reads all three shards.' Update Scope accordingly: `main/{raw_verdicts_r{1,2,3}.jsonl, manifest.json}` and `<candidate>/{raw_verdicts_r{1,2,3}.jsonl, ...}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `source='local'` (or set `REBAR_GATE_SOURCE=local`) in all `review_plan` harness invocations (Def 3 and 'The replay' section), and note that local mode satisfies 'code grounding reads the real checkout' while also preventing code-SHA variance across the N=3 replays.
evidence: Plan: 'With REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=clone (wins verbatim — config.py:116) and REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off, run review_plan(tid, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)' — no source= or REBAR_GATE_SOURCE specified
Plan: 'code grounding still reads the real checkout'

gate_source.py:93-100: `resolve_gate_handle` calls `materialize_tickets()` (git fetch) in attested mode; gate_source.py:116-117: local mode just `yield`s, leaving code root unset → reads the live checkout
gate_source.py:74-77: default source is SOURCE_ATTESTED unless REBAR_GATE_SOURCE=local is set
REBAR_SYNC_PUSH controls push (not mentioned in gate_source.py); it does not suppress code snapshot fetching
findings.finding: The plan instructs the harness to call `review_plan(tid, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)` but never specifies `source='local'` (or `REBAR_GATE_SOURCE=local`). In the default attested mode, `review_plan` calls `gate_source.resolve_gate_handle` → `materialize_tickets()`, which performs a git fetch to produce a pinned code/ticket snapshot. This means: (a) the ~230 replay runs each require network/git access not mentioned in the design; (b) if commits land between the N=3 FP-control replays, each replay may pin a different code SHA, adding an uncontrolled code-state variance source on top of the LLM nondeterminism the N=3 design is specifically trying to measure; and (c) the plan's own statement 'code grounding still reads the real checkout' is only true in local source mode (gate_source.py:116-117: local mode yields without setting code root), not attested mode. The confound is particularly sharp for the precise tier where the null-floor measurement should isolate LLM variance from all other sources.
findings.location: Def 3 (Recall-stripped clone) — 'run `review_plan(tid, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)`'; 'The replay (candidate side, both tiers)'; 'Precise tier — fresh current/main gate'
scenarios: N=3 FP-control replays run over several hours while commits land in the repo — each replay materializes a different code SHA, causing code-grounding differences that inflate or deflate the null-floor measurement, making the nondeterminism floor unreliable.
Batch harness runs in a CI/offline environment without git fetch access — attested mode fails immediately at materialize_tickets(), breaking all ~230 runs.

Candidate vs. current runs pin the same code SHA (if run back-to-back) but a later main-gate run pins a different SHA — the candidate-vs-current comparison conflates rubric change with code-state change.
findings.suggested_fix: In Def 3, change the `review_plan` call to `review_plan(tid, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True, source='local')` (or document `REBAR_GATE_SOURCE=local` as a required env var for the harness process). Add a note that local mode is required because: (1) it avoids git fetch in batch replay, (2) it guarantees all N=3 replays read the identical code state, and (3) REBAR_TRACKER_DIR-based recall stripping works correctly in both modes but local mode removes the materialized-snapshot code drift variable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC3's JSONL schema proving command to validate ALL rows, not just the last — replace `jq -e 'EXPR' criterion_deltas.jsonl` with a pattern that fails if any row fails, e.g. `jq -e 'select((has("ticket_id") and has("criterion") and has("baseline_surfaced_count") and has("candidate_surfaced_count") and has("baseline_blocking_count") and has("candidate_blocking_count") and has("net_surfaced_delta") and has("net_blocking_delta") and (.new_norm_ids|type=="array") and (.removed_norm_ids|type=="array")) | not) | error' criterion_deltas.jsonl` (exits non-zero on first failing row) or mirror AC1's `select→head-c1→!grep` pattern.
evidence: jq -e on JSONL applies the filter per input document and sets exit status based on the LAST output only (jq 1.6 behavior); any non-last document producing `false` is silently ignored
criterion_deltas.jsonl is explicitly a JSONL file (one row per (ticket_id, criterion) pair, ~200×criteria rows total) — early-row violations are not caught
AC1 correctly avoids this by using the select→head-c1→!grep pattern for its JSONL check; AC3 does not
findings.finding: AC3's schema-validation proving command uses `jq -e 'EXPR' criterion_deltas.jsonl` on a JSONL file (multiple top-level objects). With jq's `-e` flag, exit status reflects only the LAST document's filter result; a schema violation in any earlier row silently exits 0, making the proving command unable to detect partial failures in the criterion_deltas schema.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (criterion_deltas schema check), proving command: `jq -e 'has("ticket_id") and ... ' docs/.../criterion_deltas.jsonl`
scenarios: An implementation bug produces criterion_deltas.jsonl rows missing 'baseline_blocking_count' for the first 90% of rows; the last row happens to be well-formed; `jq -e` exits 0 and AC3 is declared passing despite widespread schema violations
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the AC3 jq command with:
`jq -e 'select((has("ticket_id") and has("criterion") and has("baseline_surfaced_count") and has("candidate_surfaced_count") and has("baseline_blocking_count") and has("candidate_blocking_count") and has("net_surfaced_delta") and has("net_blocking_delta") and (.new_norm_ids|type=="array") and (.removed_norm_ids|type=="array")) | not)' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas.jsonl | head -c1 | ( ! grep -q . )`
This mirrors AC1's proven-correct negative-selection pattern: select FAILING rows, assert output is empty.

findings.finding: The `jq -e '.segments[] | ... == ...'` proving command in AC2 exits 0 (pass) even when an intermediate segment fails, because `jq -e` sets exit code based solely on the LAST output value. If the last segment's consistency check passes but an earlier segment's does not, the AC silently succeeds while a matrix segment is internally inconsistent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (transition matrix proving command): `jq -e '.segments[] | (.cells.pass_to_pass + .cells.pass_to_block + .cells.block_to_pass + .cells.block_to_block + .excluded.total) == .observed_total'`
findings.suggested_fix: `jq -e '[.segments[] | (.cells.pass_to_pass + .cells.pass_to_block + .cells.block_to_pass + .cells.block_to_block + .excluded.total) == .observed_total] | all' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/transition_matrix.json`

findings.finding: The `jq -e 'has(...) and ...' criterion_deltas.jsonl` proving command in AC3 processes JSONL line-by-line and exits based on the LAST line's result. If any intermediate row is missing a required key, the AC silently passes as long as the final row is well-formed.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (criterion_deltas proving command): `jq -e 'has("ticket_id") and has("criterion") and ... and (.removed_norm_ids|type=="array")' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas.jsonl`
findings.suggested_fix: `jq -es '[.[] | has("ticket_id") and has("criterion") and has("baseline_surfaced_count") and has("candidate_surfaced_count") and has("baseline_blocking_count") and has("candidate_blocking_count") and has("net_surfaced_delta") and has("net_blocking_delta") and (.new_norm_ids|type=="array") and (.removed_norm_ids|type=="array")] | all' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas.jsonl`

findings.finding: `criterion_deltas_summary.json` is a named deliverable (described in def 6 as 'A rollup criterion_deltas_summary.json aggregates the same fields per criterion' and listed in the Scope file inventory under `<candidate>/`) but no AC verifies its existence, schema, or content.
findings.location: What § def 6 ('criterion_deltas_summary.json'); Scope file inventory; Acceptance Criteria (AC3 covers only `criterion_deltas.jsonl` and the golden test)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 with a jq one-liner: `jq -e 'has("criterion") and has("net_surfaced_delta") and has("net_blocking_delta")' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas_summary.json` (schema check) and a pytest golden that asserts the rollup aggregates correctly from the per-ticket rows.

findings.finding: The plan describes `criterion_deltas_summary.json` as a distinct committed artifact in both Scope ('per-candidate <candidate>/{…criterion_deltas_summary.json}') and def 6 ('A rollup criterion_deltas_summary.json aggregates the same fields per criterion'), but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this file's existence, schema, or correctness — it is an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: Scope section (criterion_deltas_summary.json) and def 6; all 7 ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 to add: `jq -e 'has("criterion") and has("baseline_surfaced_count") and has("candidate_surfaced_count") and has("net_blocking_delta")' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas_summary.json` and add a `pytest tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py -k summary -q` golden test covering the rollup aggregation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `replay_id` field (integer 0–2) to every entry in raw_verdicts.jsonl (and document it in the schema), OR partition into per-replay files (`raw_verdicts_r0.jsonl`, etc.), so that `e3_metrics.fp_control_eval` can group entries by replay when computing medians and pairwise null-floor comparisons.
evidence: Plan: 'run candidate-fresh ×3 and current-fresh ×3 over the recall-stripped clones' and 'R1 metrics are taken at the median across the 3 candidate replays' — median requires per-replay grouping.
Plan: raw_verdicts.jsonl for the candidate — 'runs/e3/<candidate>/raw_verdicts.jsonl' — single file, no mention of replay_id field or separate replay-1/2/3 files.

Plan: 'null-replay noise floor is current-vs-current: the median (over the 3 current replays, pairwise) count of plans gaining a new blocking finding between two current-gate runs on identical clone input' — pairwise comparison also requires replay-identity.
Absence: the raw_verdicts.jsonl schema (capturing 'verdict, material_fingerprint, full findings[]') lists no replay_id key.
findings.finding: The N=3 replays for both candidate-fresh and current-fresh (precise tier) are stated to write to a single shared file (`runs/e3/<candidate>/raw_verdicts.jsonl` / `runs/e3/main/raw_verdicts.jsonl`), but no `replay_id` or per-replay file partitioning is specified; computing per-replay medians for `new_blocking_median` and `new_advisory_median_delta` therefore requires replay-identity information that is absent from the stated schema.
findings.location: What section — 'Precise tier — fresh current/main gate'; 'LLM nondeterminism control (precise tier)' (def 7); Scope — runs/e3/ file list
scenarios: The driver completes 3 replays writing to one file; the analyze step cannot reconstruct per-replay groups to compute medians, making fp_control.json uncomputable from the committed file alone.
If checkpoint/resume restarts mid-replay-2, the driver may re-run entries from replay-1 without knowing replay-1 is complete, mixing replay groups.
findings.suggested_fix: Append `replay_id: int` to the raw_verdicts row schema; the driver increments replay_id (0, 1, 2) per full pass over the 30-plan subset. The checkpoint/resume key becomes `(ticket_id, replay_id)`. This also makes the completion assertion precise: `len({(tid, rid) for row in raw_verdicts}) == 30 × 3`.

findings.finding: The pairing protocol between the N=3 candidate replays and the N=3 current/main replays for computing per-ticket `new_advisories(t)` (and hence `new_advisory_median_delta`) and the per-ticket blocking-gain count is unspecified. The plan says metrics are 'taken at the median across the 3 candidate replays' and defines `new_advisories(t)` as candidate advisory findings minus current advisory findings — but with 3 candidate runs and 3 current runs, the plan never says which current run each candidate run is paired against (fixed 1-to-1 e.g. run1cand vs run1curr, or each candidate vs the median of all currents, or each candidate vs each current producing 9 pairs).
findings.location: Def 7 (LLM nondeterminism control, precise tier); `fp_control.json` schema; `e3_metrics.fp_control_eval`

findings.finding: INDETERMINATE verdict handling in the precise tier (30-plan FP-control) is unspecified. The plan fully specifies INDETERMINATE exclusion for the coarse tier (in the 'Verdict → matrix mapping' section) but the precise-tier definition (Def 7) and the material-mismatch fallback (Def 8) are both silent on what `fp_control_eval` should do when a fresh candidate-gate or current/main-gate run returns `verdict == 'INDETERMINATE'` for one of the 30 tickets in one of the N=3 replays.
findings.location: Def 7 (precise tier / LLM nondeterminism control); Def 8 (material-mismatch fallback); `fp_control.json` schema
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the coarse-tier rule: exclude INDETERMINATE-verdict tickets from the precise-tier per-replay metrics and add `precise_indeterminate_count` to `fp_control.json`; add a validity guard (e.g., >20% of the 30 INDETERMINATE in any single replay invalidates that replay) so the executor has a clear re-run trigger.

findings.finding: The null-floor computation protocol for `null_floor_pass_to_block` is referenced but never fully specified: the plan states it is 'the median (over the 3 current replays, pairwise) count of plans gaining a new blocking finding between two current-gate runs on identical clone input', but does not define which pairwise comparisons to take (C(3,2)=3 unordered pairs, or 3 consecutive pairs, or all 3×3 ordered pairs), nor how the result is used in the R1 decision (the plan says the 'blocking-flip count is read against this floor' but gives no comparison rule or pass/fail modifier). The `fp_control_eval` function must implement this but has no schema or decision rule to follow.
findings.location: Def 7 (LLM nondeterminism control) / AC: '30-plan FP-control result' jq probe — `null_floor_pass_to_block` field
findings.suggested_fix: In def 7, add: 'The null floor is the median across C(3,2)=3 unordered pairs of the 3 current-gate replays of #{plans gaining ≥1 new blocking finding between the two runs in that pair}. It is reported for context only — r1_pass uses the fixed 3/30 bar, not the floor — but if null_floor >= new_blocking_median, an explanatory note is appended to fp_control.json.'

findings.finding: The precise-tier design runs both candidate and main gates ×N=3 replicas but directs all three runs to a single file path (`runs/e3/main/raw_verdicts.jsonl` / `runs/e3/<candidate>/raw_verdicts.jsonl`). The plan does not specify how individual replays are tagged or separated within that file, yet fp_control.json is expected to contain `.replays|length==3`. Without a per-replay discriminator key in each row, the three runs' verdicts are indistinguishable in the file, making the median-across-replays computation impossible or error-prone.
findings.location: What / Precise tier — fresh current/main gate; Acceptance Criteria / fp_control.json AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add `replay_index: int` to each row written during the precise-tier runs; the analyze command groups `raw_verdicts.jsonl` rows by `replay_index` before computing per-replay metrics, then takes the median across the 3 groups.

findings.finding: The `null_floor_pass_to_block` computation protocol — described as 'the median (over the 3 current replays, pairwise) count of plans gaining a new blocking finding between two current-gate runs' — is underspecified at two levels that `e3_metrics.fp_control_eval` must resolve without guidance: (a) directionality: for each of the 3 pairs (r1×r2, r1×r3, r2×r3) the plan does not specify which replay is 'before' and which is 'after' (a plan gaining a blocking finding in r2 that was absent in r1 is not the same event as r1→r2 vs r2→r1, and the gate-noise concept implies a specific directional reading); (b) aggregation: the plan says 'median' but never specifies whether the 3 pairwise counts are collected and the median of those 3 counts is taken, or whether each pair produces a per-ticket set and those sets are unioned/intersected before counting, before a single median is taken over tickets.
findings.location: Def 7 (LLM nondeterminism control / precise tier); `fp_control.json` schema; `e3_metrics.fp_control_eval`
findings.suggested_fix: Recommend: treat each unordered pair {r_i, r_j} symmetrically — a plan's blocking finding is 'gained' if it appears in either run but not both (symmetric difference of blocking norm_ids), count plans with ≥1 such gain per pair, take median of the 3 counts. Specify this in Def 7 and add a golden test fixture that exercises the 3-pair aggregation explicitly.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: The plan defines routing_sha as 'sha256 of the active criteria_routing.json' (a raw file hash), but the closest existing production analogue is manifest.registry_version(), which hashes a derived JSON object (det+llm+grounded id sets + routing JSON + overlay dimensions, truncated to 16 hex chars) and is overlay-aware. The plan never specifies whether the driver should use registry_version() or implement a literal sha256(file-bytes) computation. If a raw file hash is implemented, a candidate whose rubric change is delivered entirely via a project overlay (.rebar/criteria_routing.json) would emit the same routing_sha as a main run from the same packaged file — defeating the manifest field's purpose of proving the candidate routing differed.
findings.location: Candidate-build verification section: "routing_sha (sha256 of the active criteria_routing.json)"
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'sha256 of the active criteria_routing.json' with 'manifest.registry_version(repo_root=<checkout_root>) — the same overlay-aware 16-char hex stamp the claim gate uses — so a routing change delivered via .rebar/criteria_routing.json is captured even when the packaged file is unchanged.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the bespoke `routing_sha` computation with a call to `rebar.llm.plan_review.manifest.registry_version()` (no `repo_root` on a no-overlay candidate branch) so the manifest records the same routing-identity token the attestation layer already uses, eliminating the NIH duplicate.
evidence: Plan text: 'routing_sha = sha256 of the file at its resolved package path, Path(rebar.llm.plan_review.__file__).parent / "criteria_routing.json"'
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/manifest.py:32 — registry_version() hashes the routing index (+ overlay) and returns a 16-hex digest, already bound in every plan-review attestation as the regver: line
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:113 — regver=registry_version(repo_root) is already the production field for 'which routing config was active'
No `criteria_routing.json` file exists at repo root; it is a package resource, so a raw file hash is not portable across install layouts the way registry_version() is
findings.finding: The plan invents a new `routing_sha` field in `manifest.json`, defined as 'sha256 of the active `criteria_routing.json`', computed by the driver via `Path(rebar.llm.plan_review.__file__).parent / "criteria_routing.json"`. But `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/manifest.py::registry_version()` already exists in production and computes exactly this hash (it serializes the effective routing index + overlay-augmented canon and returns a 16-hex-char SHA-256 digest). `registry_version()` is already the canonical 'which criteria-routing config was active' identifier — it is what attestations bind (`regver:` manifest line) and what the gate reads to detect registry drift. Rolling a bespoke raw-file-hash alongside it creates a duplicate, divergent signal with no stated advantage over simply calling `registry_version()` (no `repo_root` needed on a clean candidate branch with no overlay).
findings.location: Candidate-build verification section / 'routing_sha' definition
scenarios: The bespoke raw sha256 of criteria_routing.json will NOT equal registry_version() because registry_version() hashes the parsed + merged routing object (including overlay + canonical id sets), not raw file bytes — so the AC that proves 'candidate build differs from main' on routing_sha may pass even when the routing is functionally identical (byte-level formatting), or diverge when it should match.
If a project overlay is active on the candidate branch, registry_version() changes but the raw packaged criteria_routing.json bytes do not — routing_sha would miss the overlay-driven change entirely.
findings.suggested_fix: In `replay_candidate.py`, replace `routing_sha = sha256(Path(...) / 'criteria_routing.json')` with `from rebar.llm.plan_review.manifest import registry_version; manifest['routing_sha'] = registry_version()`. The AC jq probe remains unchanged (it only checks `has('routing_sha')`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Candidate-build verification section that `routing_sha` covers only the PACKAGED `criteria_routing.json` (not the `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` project overlay), and either add a `overlay_sha` field or note that R1's rubric change must be in the packaged file for this proof to hold.
evidence: Plan text: 'this identifies the candidate build's ACTIVE routing config, wherever the build is installed'
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/manifest.py:39: `EFFECTIVE routing (packaged ⊕ the '.rebar/criteria_routing.json' overlay)` — `registry_version()` is overlay-aware
src/rebar/llm/criteria/overlay.py:74-109: overlay discovery reads `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` and merges it with the packaged index
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py:160-170: packaged routing loaded via `resources.files('rebar.llm.plan_review').joinpath('criteria_routing.json')`, not via `__file__`
findings.finding: The plan asserts `routing_sha = sha256(Path(rebar.llm.plan_review.__file__).parent / 'criteria_routing.json')` identifies 'the candidate build's ACTIVE routing config', but the actual active routing is `effective_routing(repo_root)` which merges the packaged file with the `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` project overlay — the packaged-file sha alone does not capture the overlay and would be identical for two builds that differ only in the project overlay.
findings.location: What / Candidate-build verification: 'routing_sha = sha256 of the file at its resolved package path, Path(rebar.llm.plan_review.__file__).parent / "criteria_routing.json" (verified present) — this identifies the candidate build's ACTIVE routing config'
scenarios: If both candidate and main run against a project with an activated project criterion in `.rebar/criteria_routing.json`, the `routing_sha` values will be identical even though the overlay is part of the active routing — the manifest would not distinguish a change in overlay between the two runs.
An executor trusting `routing_sha` to prove 'rubric edits were genuinely in effect' could be misled when the R1 rubric edit is expressed as a project overlay rather than a packaged change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `Path(rebar.llm.plan_review.__file__).parent / 'criteria_routing.json'` access in the `routing_sha` computation with `importlib.resources.files('rebar.llm.plan_review').joinpath('criteria_routing.json').read_bytes()` (matching how the production codebase reads this resource), or delegate to the already-existing `manifest.registry_version()` — and remove the 'verified present' assertion since it is install-mode-dependent.
evidence: Plan states: 'routing_sha = sha256 of the file at its resolved package path, Path(rebar.llm.plan_review.__file__).parent / "criteria_routing.json" (verified present)'
Production code at registry.py:165-170 uses `resources.files('rebar.llm.plan_review').joinpath(_ROUTING_RESOURCE).read_text()` — never `Path(__file__).parent`
manifest.registry_version() at manifest.py:32-77 already hashes the routing content deterministically via the same importlib.resources path
Evidence class: Partially-verified — the file exists as a resource but the specific __file__-relative access surface is unconfirmed for all install modes
findings.finding: The plan computes `routing_sha` by reading `criteria_routing.json` via `Path(rebar.llm.plan_review.__file__).parent / 'criteria_routing.json'`, but the production codebase exclusively accesses this package resource through `importlib.resources.files('rebar.llm.plan_review').joinpath(...)` (`registry.py:166`). Direct `__file__`-relative path access is unreliable in editable installs, namespace packages, or zip-imported packages — the driver may fail at runtime with a `FileNotFoundError` or silently read a stale cached copy. Additionally, `manifest.registry_version()` (`manifest.py:32`) already computes a deterministic hash of the same routing content via the correct resource API, which the driver could call instead.
findings.location: What — Candidate-build verification section: 'routing_sha = sha256 of the file at its resolved package path, Path(rebar.llm.plan_review.__file__).parent / "criteria_routing.json" (verified present)'
scenarios: In a namespace-package or zip-imported install, `Path(rebar.llm.plan_review.__file__).parent / 'criteria_routing.json'` raises FileNotFoundError, making the manifest write fail or write a null routing_sha
In an editable install with a src layout, __file__ resolves correctly, but this is install-mode-dependent — the plan asserts 'verified present' without qualifying the install mode
findings.suggested_fix:
In `replay_candidate.py`, compute routing_sha as:
```python
import hashlib, importlib.resources as ir
data = ir.files('rebar.llm.plan_review').joinpath('criteria_routing.json').read_bytes()
routing_sha = hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest()
```
Or simply call `from rebar.llm.plan_review.manifest import registry_version; routing_sha = registry_version()` which already produces a stable, overlay-aware routing stamp via the correct resource API.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify checkpoint/resume and a completion assertion (`len(raw_verdicts) == 30`) for each of the N=3 precise-tier replays (both candidate and main/current), mirroring the coarse-tier's existing guarantee.
evidence: Coarse tier: 'atomic append per ticket (checkpoint/resume) with a len(raw_verdicts) == len(sample) completion assertion before any summary is written'
Precise tier: 'run candidate-fresh ×3 and current-fresh ×3 over the recall-stripped clones' — no checkpoint/resume or completion guard mentioned.
ABSENCE: no fallback or retry specification for partial precise-tier replay failures.
findings.finding: The precise-tier N=3 replication runs (both candidate and main/current gate) lack a checkpoint/resume or completion-assertion mechanism, unlike the coarse-tier which specifies 'atomic append per ticket (checkpoint/resume) with a `len(raw_verdicts) == len(sample)` completion assertion'. If a precise-tier replay fails mid-run, there is no specified recovery protocol.
findings.location: What § — 'Precise tier — fresh current/main gate' and def 7 'LLM nondeterminism control (precise tier)'
scenarios: A precise-tier replay fails after 15/30 tickets due to LLM outage; without a completion guard, a partial raw_verdicts.jsonl might be silently used to compute fp_control.json metrics.
The R1 PASS/FAIL decision could be made on incomplete data if one of 3 replays is partial.

findings.finding: The driver's recall-strip logic (deleting `*-REVIEW_RESULT.json` files in the clone) is the single most load-bearing mechanism in the design — a silent bug here (wrong glob, wrong directory level, env-var not propagated to the subprocess) would corrupt the recall-neutralization without any CI signal — yet `test_e3_metrics.py` covers only the pure `e3_metrics` functions, and no test for the driver's clone-setup/strip/manifest path is specified.
findings.location: Scope section — `harnesses/replay_candidate.py`; AC 5 (manifest proving); def 3 (recall-stripped clone)
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_e3_metrics.py` (or a sibling `test_replay_driver.py`), add a fixture that creates a tmp dir mimicking `.tickets-tracker/<ticket_id>/` with planted `*-REVIEW_RESULT.json` files, calls the clone-and-strip helper directly (extract it as a pure function `prepare_clone(src, dst, ticket_ids) -> Path`), and asserts no `*-REVIEW_RESULT.json` files remain under the cloned ticket dirs. This is LLM-free and CI-safe.

findings.finding: The plan instructs the driver to capture `routing_sha` as 'sha256 of the active `criteria_routing.json`', but `criteria_routing.json` is a Python package resource inside `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` — it has no standalone file the driver can hash, and no production code path computes such a raw sha256. The correct production analog is `manifest.registry_version(repo_root=<checkout>)`, which is overlay-aware (hashes packaged routing ⊕ `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` overlay). If a candidate's rubric edit is delivered via the project overlay rather than edits to the packaged file, a raw-file `routing_sha` would be identical between the candidate and main manifests, making the AC proving 'candidate build differs from main' silently pass even when the candidate's effective criteria routing is unchanged.
findings.location: Candidate-build verification section; AC #6 (`manifest.json` proving command)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Candidate-build verification section, change `routing_sha` definition to: `routing_sha` = `manifest.registry_version(repo_root=<candidate_checkout_root>)` (imported from `rebar.llm.plan_review.manifest`). Update the AC jq filter to compare `.routing_sha` as a 16-hex-char string. The proving command for 'candidate differs from main' already works correctly once the field is populated with the overlay-aware hash, since rubric edits change effective routing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify per-ticket exception handling in the batch driver: wrap each gate call in a try/except that catches non-INDETERMINATE exceptions, logs them, records the ticket in the `excluded` block with `reason: exception`, and continues — or define a max-retry-then-exclude policy.
evidence: Plan states: 'A candidate verdict that is INDETERMINATE (LLM outage / mid-run degradation — §5.1: ~8% of reviews)... is excluded from the matrix' — but does not specify that exceptions from the gate call are caught and mapped to INDETERMINATE/excluded rather than propagating and crashing the driver.

Checkpoint/resume recovers the batch on restart, but a repeating exception on one ticket would make that ticket permanently unprocessable, stalling the run without any stated fallback (skip after N retries, log and exclude).
Absence: no retry policy, no per-ticket try/except-as-excluded, no max-retry-then-skip is described.
findings.finding: The batch driver has no specified per-ticket exception handling: if a single ticket's LLM gate call raises a Python exception (network timeout, API 5xx not caught by the gate layer, etc.) rather than returning an INDETERMINATE verdict, there is no stated catch-as-excluded logic to prevent the driver from crashing and leaving that ticket unrecorded in either raw_verdicts or the excluded block.
findings.location: What section — 'The replay (candidate side, both tiers)'; harnesses/replay_candidate.py (driver)
scenarios: A transient API timeout on one ticket raises an unhandled exception; the driver crashes; on resume it retries the same ticket; if the error is persistent, the run never completes.
An API response is malformed (not INDETERMINATE, just unparseable JSON); the gate raises ValueError; driver crashes mid-batch.
findings.suggested_fix: In replay_candidate.py, wrap each `review_plan(...)` call in a try/except block; on any exception (after optional 1-2 retries with exponential backoff), append `{ticket_id, excluded: true, reason: 'exception', error: str(e)}` to raw_verdicts.jsonl and continue. This makes the excluded block complete and the run self-healing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the recall-stripped clone is created with `tempfile.mkdtemp()` (unique per run invocation) and is removed with `shutil.rmtree` in a `finally` block after the run completes or fails, and document this in the driver spec.
evidence: Plan: 'copy REAL_REPO/.tickets-tracker → a tmp store, then delete every *-REVIEW_RESULT.json file under each sampled ticket's dir in the clone' — 'tmp store' location is unspecified (fixed path vs. tempfile.mkdtemp()).
Plan: 'The real .tickets-tracker branch/origin are untouched' — confirms isolation intent but says nothing about cleanup.
Absence: no `shutil.rmtree` or equivalent cleanup step is described, and no unique-per-run naming convention (e.g., uuid-suffixed tmpdir) is stated.
Plan: N=3 replays for candidate + N=3 for main could run sequentially or concurrently; if the clone path is fixed, concurrent runs corrupt each other's clone.
findings.finding: The recall-stripped clone (a copy of `.tickets-tracker` with REVIEW_RESULT files deleted) has no specified creation path (fixed path vs. unique temp dir) and no retirement/cleanup path; concurrent or repeated runs could collide on a fixed clone path, and the clone accumulates indefinitely in the filesystem.
findings.location: Definitions — def 3 'Recall-stripped clone (both tiers)'; harnesses/replay_candidate.py (driver)
scenarios: Operator runs `replay_candidate.py run --candidate r1` twice simultaneously (e.g., CI parallelism); both copy to the same fixed clone path; the second deletes REVIEW_RESULT files while the first is mid-run, producing inconsistent recall-stripping.
After 10 candidate evaluations, 10 orphaned clone directories accumulate in tmp, each containing a full copy of the ticket store.
findings.suggested_fix: In replay_candidate.py: `clone_dir = tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix='e3_clone_'); try: ... finally: shutil.rmtree(clone_dir, ignore_errors=True)`. For the N=3 precise-tier replays, create a fresh clone per replay (or re-delete REVIEW_RESULT files between replays from the same clone, documented explicitly).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground the claim that `review_plan()` returns `material_fingerprint` in the verdict dict by citing the specific `__init__.py` / `pass1.py` line(s) that include it in the return value (not just compute it for the sidecar).
evidence: "Drive the candidate branch build's gate over the sample … capturing each returned verdict dict — `verdict`, `material_fingerprint`, full `findings[]`"
The plan cites `pass1.py:551` to ground that `orchestrator.material_fingerprint` IS computed, but does not cite any line showing it is included in the dict returned by `review_plan()` to the caller.
If `material_fingerprint` is only written into the sidecar (not returned), the driver can never compare fresh fingerprints to the frozen baseline, silently voiding the mismatch exclusion.
findings.finding: `review_plan()` returning `material_fingerprint` in its verdict dict is asserted but not code-grounded; the entire drift-control mechanism (def 4, def 8, AC1) depends on this key being present in the returned dict, not only in the sidecar payload.
findings.location: What > def 4 (plan-text drift control) / def 3 (fresh gate run capture) / AC line 1
scenarios: Driver captures returned dict and reads `.material_fingerprint`; if the key is absent, fingerprint comparison always fails or raises KeyError, disabling the drift guard for every ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a cite like `__init__.py:NNN` showing the return dict construction includes `material_fingerprint`, or — if it does not — note that the driver must read it from `sidecar.latest_review_result()` on the clone immediately after the run, and update the recall-strip protocol accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and cite the `sidecar.py` line(s) that establish `all_review_results(tid)` returns rounds newest→oldest (or add an explicit `.sort(key=..., reverse=True)` call in the driver to make the ordering self-contained).
evidence: "returns every retained round newest→oldest" — stated as a fact but no file:line grounding is provided for the ordering contract.

Baseline round selection logic ("the most-recent round whose `impact_model_version == 'plan-v3'`") is entirely predicated on this ordering; if the function returns oldest→newest or unsorted, the wrong round is silently selected as the baseline.
findings.finding: `sidecar.all_review_results(tid)` returning rounds in newest-to-oldest order is asserted as the basis for "most-recent round" baseline selection, but no code line is cited to confirm this ordering guarantee.
findings.location: What > def 2 (Baseline verdict source, coarse tier)
scenarios: If `all_review_results` returns oldest-first, the driver picks the earliest rather than most-recent `plan-v3` round, producing a stale baseline that may have a different `material_fingerprint` and verdict than the true most-recent baseline.
findings.suggested_fix: Cite `sidecar.py:NNN` confirming the ordering, or make the driver's "pick most-recent" logic explicit with `max(rounds, key=lambda r: r['timestamp'])` so it is independent of return order.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete `ticket_type` vocabulary (enumerate the valid values from `outcome_corpus.jsonl`/README schema) and specify the sub-floor cell collapse rule precisely (e.g., 'merge the minority verdict cell into the majority verdict cell for that ticket_type').
evidence: 'Draw ~200 stratified by (ticket_type × baseline_verdict), proportional to the pool with a floor of 5 per non-empty cell (sub-floor cells collapse into the same ticket_type's larger verdict cell)'
No definition of `ticket_type` vocabulary appears anywhere in the plan.
ABSENCE: the plan does not cite `outcome_corpus.jsonl` schema or `runs/README.md:53-88` for `ticket_type` values, so an executor cannot enumerate the cells without reading external material.
findings.finding: The `ticket_type` vocabulary used for stratified sampling is never defined. The plan says 'stratified by (ticket_type × baseline_verdict)' with a 'floor of 5 per non-empty cell' and 'sub-floor cells collapse into the same ticket_type's larger verdict cell', but the full set of `ticket_type` values (the cell labels) is never enumerated, nor is the collapse direction/procedure precisely defined.
findings.location: What § — 'Stratified sampling frame (reproducible)'
scenarios: Executor implementing `replay_candidate.py sample` cannot build the stratification grid without knowing the ticket_type enum values.
The floor-of-5 collapse logic is ambiguous: 'same ticket_type's larger verdict cell' — does PASS absorb BLOCK or vice versa when BLOCK has <5?
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pinned definition: 'ticket_type ∈ {"feature", "bug", "chore", ...} (from outcome_corpus.jsonl field `ticket_type`; full vocabulary: <list>)' and clarify collapse as 'sub-floor cells are pooled into the PASS cell of the same ticket_type for sampling purposes'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `mismatch_count` in `fp_control.json` is the per-replay value (implying an array of 3 values) or an aggregate (e.g., max across replays), and clarify that the invalidity guard triggers if the max across replays exceeds 20%.
evidence: 'If > 20% of the 30-plan subset mismatches in any replay (mismatch_count/30 > 0.20), that FP-control run is INVALID'
'has("mismatch_count")' — single scalar in the AC schema
ABSENCE: the plan does not define whether mismatch_count in fp_control.json is per-replay (array) or aggregate.
findings.finding: The `mismatch_count` field in `fp_control.json` is singular (the AC jq checks `has("mismatch_count")`), but the N=3 replays may each have a different mismatch count. The plan never specifies which replay's mismatch_count is recorded, nor how the 20%-invalidity guard is applied across replays — is each replay checked independently, or is the maximum/median across replays used?
findings.location: What § — def 8 'Material-mismatch fallback + FP-control validity guard'; AC item 5 fp_control.json jq assertion
scenarios: Replay 1 has 4 mismatches (valid), Replay 2 has 7 (invalid) — the guard triggers on replay 2 but fp_control.json stores a single value, making it unclear whether the overall run is valid or which value to store.
The `e3_metrics.fp_control_valid` function signature is `fp_control_valid(mismatch_count, n=30, max_frac=0.20)` but it's unclear how the caller aggregates across 3 replays before calling it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'fp_control.json stores mismatch_count as an array of 3 per-replay counts; fp_control_valid is called on each independently and fp_control_valid = True only if all 3 are valid (max_frac ≤ 0.20 in each replay).' Update the AC jq to assert `(.mismatch_count | type == "array" and length == 3)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete `excluded` block schema in `transition_matrix.json` — specify whether it has sub-keys per exclusion reason (e.g., `indeterminate`, `mismatch`) in addition to `total`, and add those keys to the AC jq assertion.
evidence: 'A candidate verdict that is INDETERMINATE … or a material-fingerprint mismatch is excluded from the matrix … counted in a separate excluded block'
AC jq: `(.excluded.total)` — only `.total` is asserted
ABSENCE: no definition of whether `excluded` has additional sub-keys per exclusion reason.
findings.finding: The `excluded` block in `transition_matrix.json` is referenced in the AC jq as `excluded.total` (for row-sum consistency), but its full schema is never defined. The plan mentions two distinct exclusion reasons (INDETERMINATE verdict and material-fingerprint mismatch), but whether `excluded` has sub-keys per reason (e.g., `excluded.indeterminate`, `excluded.mismatch`) or only a flat `total` is unspecified — leaving `e3_metrics.build_matrix`'s output contract ambiguous.
findings.location: What § — def on 'Verdict → matrix mapping (coarse tier)'; AC item 2 transition_matrix.json jq assertion
scenarios: An executor implementing `build_matrix` cannot know whether to populate `excluded.indeterminate` and `excluded.mismatch` sub-keys or just `excluded.total`, leading to inconsistent output across implementors.
If a consumer of transition_matrix.json wants to debug high exclusion rates, a flat `total` gives no diagnostic signal.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the schema definition: 'excluded: {total: int, indeterminate: int, mismatch: int}' and update the AC jq to assert both sub-keys are present and sum to total.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pinned fp_control.json field-name table to What (analogous to def 6's 10-key criterion_deltas schema), explicitly defining `new_blocking_median` as 'median over N replays of (count of plans in the 30-plan subset that gain ≥1 new blocking finding candidate-vs-current)' and all other AC5-referenced keys (`replays`, `new_advisory_median_delta`, `null_floor_pass_to_block`, `mismatch_count`, `fp_control_valid`, `r1_pass`).
evidence: "median candidate-vs-current #{plans gaining ≥1 new blocking finding} **> 3/30**"
"jq -e '(.replays|length==3) and has(\"new_blocking_median\") ... and (.r1_pass == ((.new_blocking_median <= 3) and (.new_advisory_median_delta <= 1)))'"
def 6 has an explicit 10-key pinned schema; no equivalent pinned schema exists for fp_control.json fields
ABSENCE: fp_control.json field names are not listed anywhere in What or Scope
findings.finding: The json key `new_blocking_median` used in AC5's jq proof (`(.new_blocking_median <= 3)`) is never defined in the What section; What def 7 defines the metric as 'median candidate-vs-current #{plans gaining ≥1 new blocking finding}' — a count of plans, not a per-plan blocking count — but the key name `new_blocking_median` could equally read as 'median of per-plan new-blocking-finding counts,' which is a different statistic. The fp_control.json schema is not pinned with field names the way criterion_deltas.jsonl is (def 6's 10-key schema), leaving an ambiguity an implementer of `e3_metrics.fp_control_eval` must resolve by inference.
findings.location: What def 7 vs AC5 jq `has('new_blocking_median')` and `.r1_pass == ((.new_blocking_median <= 3) and (.new_advisory_median_delta <= 1))`
scenarios: An implementer reading def 7 might store the median-over-replays of (per-plan blocking-finding count) rather than the median-over-replays of (count-of-plans-with-≥1-new-blocking-finding), satisfying the jq key check while computing a different statistic.
The golden test (`pytest -k 'fp_control or valid'`) is the only other spec artifact; without a field-name table the test fixture drives the schema silently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which build's `sidecar.norm_id` governs the offline `analyze` step (e.g., require main-branch `rebar` installed for analysis, or pre-compute and commit norm_ids into `raw_verdicts.jsonl` at run time so analysis is build-agnostic).
evidence: 'every proving command below re-derives the summaries offline from committed raw_verdicts.jsonl + baseline.jsonl'
'the driver imports from rebar.llm.plan_review import sidecar and computes sidecar.norm_id(f) for every finding on BOTH sides uniformly'
Plan never specifies which build version of sidecar is active when `analyze --candidate <label>` runs offline — absence of build-pinning for the analysis step.
raw_verdicts.jsonl carries 'full findings[]' (raw dicts), not pre-computed norm_ids, so norm_id is re-evaluated at analysis time.
findings.finding: The offline `analyze` step re-computes `sidecar.norm_id(f)` for all findings using whatever `rebar` build is active in the analysis environment, but the plan never specifies which build (main vs. candidate) must be installed during analysis. If `norm_id` semantics change between the main and a candidate branch (a realistic scenario for R-candidates that edit the Pass-1 finder rubric), re-running `analyze --candidate <label>` under different installed builds produces different `criterion_deltas.jsonl` outputs from the same committed `raw_verdicts.jsonl`, silently breaking reproducibility of the committed summaries.
findings.location: Scope / `e3_metrics.py`; def 6 (per-criterion finding delta schema); AC3 proving command
scenarios: Executor runs `analyze --candidate r1` with main branch installed → one criterion_deltas.jsonl. Later re-runs with r1 branch installed → different norm_ids → different deltas from identical raw data. The committed summary is not reproducible across environments.
A future candidate changes norm_id's criterion-bucketing logic; analysis of a prior candidate's raw_verdicts silently produces incorrect deltas when re-run against the new build.
findings.suggested_fix: At run time (during the fresh gate replay), augment each finding dict written to `raw_verdicts.jsonl` with a pre-computed `norm_id` field (e.g., `f['norm_id'] = sidecar.norm_id(f)` before appending). The `analyze` step then reads the committed norm_ids directly rather than recomputing them, making offline re-derivation fully reproducible regardless of which build is active.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `sidecar.py` line citation for `norm_id` from `sidecar.py:247-257` to `sidecar.py:261-269` (the actual function definition location).
evidence: Plan text: 'the exact-text `id` differs across builds by construction (sidecar.py:247-257)'

Actual code: `def norm_id(finding: dict[str, Any]) -> str:` is at sidecar.py line 261 (verified by Read); lines 247-258 are the comment block `# ── normalized finding fingerprint (OBSERVABILITY-ONLY...`
The function definition is at lines 261-269, not 247-257
findings.finding: The plan's inline cite `(sidecar.py:247-257)` for the `norm_id` function definition is factually incorrect — `def norm_id(...)` is at line 261, not 247-257; lines 247-258 contain only the comment block preceding the function.
findings.location: What / def 6 / Per-criterion finding delta schema: '...reword-tolerant, criterion-scoped fingerprint (`pass1.py:506`), correct because the exact-text `id` differs across builds by construction (sidecar.py:247-257)'
scenarios: An executor or auditor trying to verify the `norm_id` implementation at the cited lines will find only a comment block, not the function — potentially causing confusion about whether the right function is being used.
findings.suggested_fix: Change '(sidecar.py:247-257)' to '(sidecar.py:261-269)' in def 6's parenthetical.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
